summary:
The Afanasievo culture, approximately 3000–2500 BC, occupied the Minusinsk Basin and the Altai-Sayan mountain region of southern Siberia and Mongolia — some of the easternmost territory associated with the expansion of steppe pastoralists from the Pontic-Caspian homeland. The culture is remarkable for its genetic and material culture affinities with the Yamna culture of the western steppe, suggesting that the Afanasievo people represent an early eastward migration of steppe populations into central Asia.

Afanasievo people practiced a semi-nomadic pastoralist economy, herding cattle, sheep, and horses in the mountain valleys and highland pastures of central Asia. Their burials, typically in round kurgans or stone enclosures, reveal a society with social hierarchies expressed through grave goods including copper and bronze ornaments, pottery, and animal sacrifices. The physical anthropology of Afanasievo individuals distinguishes them clearly from the contemporaneous forager populations of northern Asia and aligns them with European steppe populations.

The Afanasievo culture has attracted attention because it represents the earliest known carrier of western steppe ancestry into the eastern half of Eurasia. Some scholars have proposed that the Afanasievo people were speakers of early Tocharian languages — the extinct Indo-European languages preserved in manuscripts from the Tarim Basin of western China — which would make this culture a key node in the spread of Indo-European languages across Eurasia. Whether or not this linguistic hypothesis is correct, the Afanasievo culture demonstrates that the eastward reach of steppe mobility was already very considerable by 3000 BC.
